notes.anchor: the third day before the Ides of April
notes.note: Ante diem tertium Idus Apriles = 11 April 43 BC; the letter is dated in the morning of 12 April (the day before the Ides), with the opening note explaining the brief supplementary postscript.

notes.anchor: the day before the Ides of April
notes.note: Pridie Idus Apriles = 12 April 43 BC; Cicero adds this supplement upon learning that Scaptius's couriers had not yet left.

notes.anchor: with Pansa opposing and angry, delivered the motion that Cassius should pursue Dolabella by war
notes.note: Consul Gaius Vibius Pansa Caetronianus, though constitutionally Cicero's superior, blocked or resisted this proposal; Cicero pushed the motion through the Senate despite consular opposition, an act of considerable political boldness.

Rhodes, a semi-autonomous island state allied with Rome, refused Dolabella entry to its harbour — an important signal of where Greek loyalties lay; this forced Dolabella to continue his eastward flight through Asia.

The Senate's decree authorized Brutus to requisition interest-free loans (pecuniae mutuae) from provincial and allied communities, a standard emergency measure; the practice was resented by the communities affected.

notes.anchor: the temper of the Caesarian party (as the party is even now called)
notes.note: Cicero's parenthetical remark is significant: even eighteen months after Caesar's murder, his faction retained its identity and name, illustrating how deeply Caesarian loyalties were embedded in Roman politics.
